critique.summary: The article presents a directory of over 135 open hardware devices that users can flash with their own firmware, featuring a variety of development boards, single-board computers, and modules suitable for IoT, embedded systems, and DIY projects. It highlights specific devices with their specifications, use cases, and approximate prices.
mainPoints: The directory lists numerous open hardware devices compatible with custom firmware flashing.
Devices include development boards, SBCs, modules, and DIY kits from various manufacturers.
Specifications such as processors, memory, connectivity options, and intended applications are provided for each device.
The platform aims to empower users to build customized hardware solutions for applications like IoT, home automation, industrial control, and more.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the availability of open hardware devices flashable with custom firmware are consistent with known open hardware ecosystems and product offerings from various manufacturers. The device specifications and use cases align with publicly available information from manufacturers and reputable electronics distributors. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting verification of the completeness or exclusivity of the listings.
considerations: The article does not provide information on the licensing or open-source compliance of the hardware or firmware.
There is no discussion of the potential challenges or technical skills required to flash custom firmware on these devices.
The directory's criteria for including devices and the frequency of updates are not mentioned.
Security implications of flashing custom firmware are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the availability and coverage of open hardware devices flashable with custom firmware, comparing the new directory to existing repositories and communities. Some comments highlight the extensive device support in other projects, while others touch on pricing and marketing aspects.

comments.author: haddonist
comments.text:
Looks good, but it'll probably take a while until it's anywhere close to the coverage of existing repositories:

https://templates.blakadder.com/ has almost 3,000 devices flashable onto Tasmota firmware.

For older Tuya devices there's https://github.com/tuya-cloudcutter/tuya-cloudcutter

OpenBeken https://github.com/openshwprojects/OpenBK7231T_App covers 800 of the newer generation Tuya devices.

And there's a large community adapting ESP32 devices onto https://esphome.io/
comments.analysis: Provides detailed and accurate references to existing large repositories, offering a well-informed perspective on the current state of device coverage.

comments.author: zdw
comments.text: There are another ~3k devices on the OpenWRT table of hardware that would fall into this category: https://toh.openwrt.org/
comments.analysis: Adds valuable information about additional hardware support from a reputable source, complementing the previous comment's data.

comments.author: randyrand
comments.text:
more often than not, you could just buy the same CPU on the open market and swap it in.

It won’t have any restrictions if it’s a fresh CPU
comments.analysis: Offers practical advice on hardware replacement and restrictions, which is generally accurate but context-dependent.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.analysis: Brief comment on pricing that is plausible but lacks detail or supporting evidence.

comments.author: Neywiny
comments.text: Feels weird to advertise a microcontroller dev board this way. But the other stuff is cool
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective opinion without substantive information or constructive feedback.
